era_context: Same as previous years: Iron Age consolidation, Egyptian Twenty-first Dynasty, early Middle Assyrian period, Levantine state formation, Greek Geometric period.

events.title: Early Iron Age technological transition in the Mediterranean
events.description: By 1044 BCE, the transition from Bronze Age to Iron Age technology was well underway across the Mediterranean and Near East. Iron was increasingly used for tools, weapons, and agricultural implements, though bronze remained important for prestige items, decorative objects, and some specialized tools. The adoption of iron technology was gradual and uneven across regions; some areas (particularly the Levant and Greece) adopted iron more rapidly, while others (Egypt, Mesopotamia) maintained bronze-working traditions longer. This technological transition was one of the defining features of the Iron Age period.

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from multiple sites across the Mediterranean and Near East

events.certainty_note: The broad pattern of iron adoption in the Iron Age is archaeologically confirmed. The year 1044 BCE falls within the documented period of this technological transition.

events.title: Stability and consolidation in the early Iron Age world
events.description: By 1044 BCE, roughly 200 years after the Late Bronze Age collapse, the world had largely stabilized into new political and economic configurations. The great empires of the Late Bronze Age (Hittites, Mycenaean Greeks, Egyptian New Kingdom) had fallen or declined, but new powers were rising (Assyria, Phoenician city-states, early Iron Age kingdoms in the Levant and Greece). Trade networks were being reestablished, settlements were becoming more stable, and cultural traditions were being reformulated in Iron Age forms. This year represents a point of relative stability within the broader Iron Age consolidation process.

sources.name: Archaeological surveys and excavations across multiple regions
Historiographic consensus on the Iron Age transition

events.certainty_note: The broad pattern of Iron Age consolidation is well supported by archaeological evidence. The year 1044 BCE is assigned as a representative point within this process, though year-specific precision is not possible.

disconfirming_evidence: No disconfirming evidence identified for events listed.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 1044 BCE is consistent with the previous years in this batch. The surviving sources are sparse and fragmentary, heavily dependent on archaeology and later compilations. No contemporary written records from most of the world exist for this specific year. The Egyptian administrative record remains the most detailed source available, but it does not provide year-specific narrative events for 1044 BCE.

graph_edges.note: Technological transition is part of the broader Iron Age consolidation.
Technological change is one aspect of broader Iron Age consolidation.
